subject: [PATCH v5 0/9] dax/kmem: atomic whole-device hotplug via sysfs

reviews.author: Gregory Price (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledges that some reviewer comments remain unaddressed and plans to re-spin the patch in version 6, after the merge window closes.
reviews.raw_body:
Disregard, there are a few unaddressed Sashiko comments, I'm just going
to respin this.  Will wait until after the merge window closes for v6.

The rough shape of things should still hold w/ prior feedback.

~Gregory

2026-06-24.patch_summary: This patch series adds atomic whole-device hotplug control to the dax kmem driver via a sysfs 'state' attribute, allowing for transitions across entire regions of memory to be performed atomically and safely. The current implementation has a race condition between offlining and removing memory blocks, which this patch aims to solve by introducing a new ABI with state names mirroring per-block memoryX/state attributes. The series also includes mm preparation changes to support the new hotplug control, including adding helpers for online type management and atomic offline+remove of multiple ranges. The dax/kmem feature is updated to plumb online_type through its device creation path and extract hotplug/hotremove into helper functions. A selftest is added to regression test the new attribute.

reviews.author: Hannes Reinecke
reviews.summary: Reviewer Hannes Reinecke questioned the need to treat 'unbind' as a given thing and suggested disallowing unbind when memory is online, recommending that admins offline memory first using the new sysfs knob.
reviews.raw_body:
That looks good, but question remains:

Why do we need to treat the 'unbind' call as a given thing?
If we know that we cannot handle online memory during unbind,
can't we just disallow unbind in that case?
I don't think it's too much to ask from an admin to offline
the memory first, _especially_ as now we have a simple knob
to do that ...

Cheers,

Hannes
-- 
Dr. Hannes Reinecke                  Kernel Storage Architect
hare@suse.de                                +49 911 74053 688
SUSE Software Solutions GmbH, Frankenstr. 146, 90461 Nrnberg
HRB 36809 (AG Nrnberg), GF: I. Totev, A. McDonald, W. Knoblich

reviews.author: Gregory Price (author)
reviews.summary: The author acknowledged that unbind is a violent operation and cannot fail, but still plans to prevent deadlocks by leaking resources in the case where 'state!=UNPLUGGED'.
reviews.raw_body:
No.  Unbind is a violent operation - unbinds cannot fail, and a
straight, uncoordinated unbind is essentially a `--force` flag:
the admin accepts the risks.

To your point, the admin either does the nice thing are they
muck up the system.

But we should still try to do something sane to defend the kernel,
in this case we should try to prevent that task from becoming
deadlocked.  The only way to do that is to leak the resources.

I'm making a small modification to this code to reinstate the
legacy behavior when "state!=UNPLUGGED".

~Gregory

reviews.author: David (Arm)
reviews.summary:
Reviewer David suggested a minor improvement to the code formatting, specifically recommending that the struct range initialization be done in a more readable way by using an initializer list.

Reviewer David pointed out that the use of 'unsigned int' for nr_ranges could lead to a condition where it is always false (!nr_ranges), and suggested adding a WARN_ON_ONCE() to handle this case.

Reviewer David (Arm) suggested that instead of breaking, the patch should warn and continue, as a clean rollback is not possible after partially removing memory.
reviews.raw_body:
I prefer this more readable as:

struct range range = {
	.start = start,
	.end = start + size - 1,
};

---

With "unsigned int" this will be !nr_ranges.

Wondering whether we would WARN_ON_ONCE() here.

---

Do we really want to break? I'd say, just warn and continue, and fake rc == 0.
Something is seriously messed up already, and we partially removed memory. There
is no clean rollback possible.

Similar to __remove_memory(), ignoring the error because it offlined it already.

reviews.author: David (Arm)
reviews.summary: Reviewer David requested that the hotplug sysfs attribute be separated into its own patch for better reviewability and maintainability.
reviews.raw_body:
... and this as a separate patch, please?

Nothing else jumped at me.

-- 
Cheers,

David

reviews.author: Gregory Price (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2.

reviews.author: Gregory Price (author)
reviews.summary:
Author acknowledged a concern about indentation in their code and joked about needing to adjust to the new style.

Author responded to David's feedback on error handling, suggesting that sending -EINVAL is sufficient instead of a WARN() when callers pass invalid arguments.

Author acknowledged that the patch contained an unnecessary refactoring and agreed to revert changes in a future version.

Author acknowledged the need for a fix and agreed to change the behavior of unbind when the device is not unplugged, also mentioning an existing corner condition that needed to be addressed.
sentiment_signals: acknowledged the need for a fix
reviews.raw_body:
ack, ack.  need to reprogram my brain to two-indent style, i keep doing
this reflexively.

---

Seems reasonable.  Do we normally WARN when callers send dumb arguments?
Seems like sending -EINVAL is sufficient?

---

No, this was mostly my way ok keeping try of what was being moved around
while working it.  I will change it back.

---

This seems reasonable, will change to warn and continue + return error.

Sashiko actually pointed out there there's a corner condition here with
offline rollback, so i needed to tweak this chunk anyway.

~Gregory

reviews.author: David (Arm)
reviews.summary: Reviewer David expressed mild concern about the WARN() flag being too lenient and potentially masking issues, but ultimately has no strong opinion on the matter.
reviews.raw_body:
We do it when we really expect no sane user to do that and doing it would
indicate an actual problem.

Like passing a nid to add_memory_resource() that doesn't even exist.

But yeah, no strong opinion here, we can just keep it as is.

-- 
Cheers,

David
